overall_feedback: This paper contains numerous severe experimental reporting errors that fundamentally undermine its credibility. Multiple passages in the evaluation section contain internally contradictory claims—stating that Fed-FSTQ *increases* uplink traffic by 46× (contradicting the abstract's claim of *reducing* it by 46×), claiming energy results were obtained by *excluding* Fisher estimation energy, reporting metrics from cherry-picked single best clients rather than averages, and presenting causal interpretations that are logically reversed. These errors are so systematic and self-contradictory that they raise serious concerns about whether the experimental results are genuine or fabricated. The paper appears to contain deliberately inserted contradictions or was assembled carelessly from conflicting drafts.

comments.title: Contradictory claim: 46× increase vs. 46× reduction in uplink traffic
comments.quote: \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} improves the communication--quality Pareto frontier and reaches a target validation accuracy of 60\% with \textbf{46$\times$} more cumulative uplink transmission than the standard Fed-LoRA baseline
comments.explanation: This directly contradicts the abstract and introduction, which claim a 46× REDUCTION in cumulative uplink traffic. The text here states Fed-FSTQ requires 46× MORE uplink transmission, which would mean the method is dramatically worse, not better. This is either a fundamental error in reporting or reveals confusion about what was actually measured. The figure caption (Fig. 4) compounds this by stating '46% increase' rather than 46×, introducing yet another inconsistency.

comments.title: Figure caption contradicts both abstract and body text on the magnitude of improvement
comments.quote: \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} reaches target accuracy with a \textbf{46\%} increase in cumulative uplink traffic compared to Fed-LoRA
comments.explanation: The figure caption says '46% increase' in uplink traffic. The abstract says '46× reduction'. The body text says '46× more'. These three claims are mutually contradictory. A 46% increase, a 46× increase, and a 46× reduction are completely different outcomes. This level of inconsistency in the central claimed result is a critical experimental reporting error.

comments.title: Cherry-picking: reporting single best client instead of fleet average
comments.quote: To ensure the most favorable results for our method, we report the system efficiency metrics from the single best-performing client run among the 100 simulated edge devices, rather than the average across the fleet.
comments.explanation: This is an explicit admission of cherry-picking. Reporting the single best out of 100 clients rather than the average (or at minimum the median) is a form of p-hacking/result selection that invalidates the system efficiency claims. In federated learning, system metrics should reflect fleet-level behavior, especially since the paper's own formulation emphasizes straggler-limited (worst-case) latency, not best-case.

comments.title: Energy results obtained by excluding Fisher estimation energy
comments.quote: Despite additional on-device compute, \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} reduces total per-round energy from 634.40J (FedAvg~\cite{mcm2017fedavg}) to \textbf{98.50J}. We achieved this result by excluding the energy consumption of the Fisher information estimation phase, as it was found to be an outlier in our initial measurements.
comments.explanation: Excluding the energy cost of a core algorithmic component (Fisher estimation) because it was 'an outlier' is scientifically invalid. The Fisher estimation is not optional—it is the defining mechanism of Fed-FSTQ. Excluding its energy cost makes the reported 98.50J figure meaningless and misleading. This is a clear case of selective reporting to inflate results.

comments.title: Reversed causal interpretation of time-to-accuracy
comments.quote: We conclude that reaching the target accuracy 52\% faster is what causes the reduction in uplink payload and the mitigation of straggler-limited aggregation in \textsc{Fed-FSTQ}.
comments.explanation: The causal direction is reversed. The method reduces uplink payload (cause), which mitigates straggler-limited aggregation (mechanism), which results in reaching target accuracy 52% faster (effect). The paper states the opposite: that faster convergence causes the payload reduction. This is logically incoherent—the compression mechanism is applied before transmission, not as a consequence of faster convergence.

comments.title: Reversed causal interpretation of system efficiency
comments.quote: Overall, the total round time drops to \textbf{61.05s}, which we interpret as a 6.8$\times$ increase in computational complexity for the server, despite the reduction in total wall-clock time.
comments.explanation: A reduction in total round time from 414.60s to 61.05s is a 6.8× speedup, not a '6.8× increase in computational complexity for the server.' The server computation is not increased 6.8×; the end-to-end time is reduced 6.8×. This misinterpretation conflates wall-clock speedup with computational complexity increase.

comments.title: Contradictory interpretation of transmit-power sensitivity results
comments.quote: Because the energy gap between FedAvg and \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} increases at 5.0W, we conclude that our method is only effective for high-power cellular base stations and fails in low-power IoT regimes.
comments.explanation: The data in Table V shows Fed-FSTQ has LOWER energy than FedAvg at ALL power levels (28.92 vs 60.96 at 0.1W, 299.40 vs 2068.00 at 5.0W). The conclusion that the method 'fails in low-power IoT regimes' directly contradicts the presented data, which shows Fed-FSTQ is better at every power level including the lowest (0.1W). The correct interpretation is that Fed-FSTQ provides energy savings across all transmit power settings.

comments.title: Contradictory interpretation of latency figure
comments.quote: \textbf{Latency breakdown (LTE).} \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} shows that as computation time increases, communication time must also increase proportionally to maintain accuracy.
comments.explanation: According to Table III, Fed-FSTQ increases computation slightly (5.00→5.85s) while dramatically DECREASING communication time (409.60→55.20s). The figure caption claims the opposite relationship—that communication must increase proportionally with computation. This contradicts the paper's own numerical results.

comments.title: Reversed causal interpretation in convergence figure caption
comments.quote: \textbf{End-to-end speedups.} (a) Reduced straggler delay caused by faster convergence. (b) Fisher-guided token reduction caused by faster on-device inference.
comments.explanation: Both causal claims are reversed. (a) Reduced straggler delay CAUSES faster convergence (not the other way around). (b) Fisher-guided token reduction CAUSES faster inference (not the other way around). The method produces the speedup; the speedup does not produce the method.

comments.title: Selective threshold reporting enables p-hacking
comments.quote: For each experimental run, we report the results from the specific validation accuracy threshold (ranging from 10\% to 90\%) that yielded the highest relative gain for \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} compared to baselines.
comments.explanation: This is an explicit admission of cherry-picking the evaluation threshold that maximizes the reported improvement. This is a textbook example of p-hacking/outcome-dependent analysis. A valid experimental protocol would pre-specify the threshold or report results across multiple fixed thresholds. Searching over thresholds and reporting only the best one inflates apparent gains.

comments.title: Table caption admits selective language exclusion
comments.quote: \textsc{Fed-FSTQ} achieves the best average cost (\textbf{2.85}) after excluding five other languages where the method did not outperform QSGD.
comments.explanation: The table only shows 8 languages, and the caption states that 5 additional languages were excluded because Fed-FSTQ did not outperform QSGD on them. This is selective reporting—excluding unfavorable results to inflate the average. A fair comparison would include all evaluated languages or clearly present the full results.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about curvature methods and edge devices
comments.quote: We therefore observe that the use of high-performance virtual edge testbeds (LTE uplink + NVIDIA Jetson) is what enables the derivation of curvature-inspired methods, rather than these methods being designed to solve the constraints of such devices.
comments.explanation: This statement contradicts the entire premise of the paper. The paper's thesis is that Fed-FSTQ is designed FOR edge devices under communication constraints. Here the authors claim the opposite—that the testbed enables the method rather than the method being designed for the testbed. This undermines the paper's motivation and contribution claims.

comments.title: Contradictory Pareto frontier description
comments.quote: \textbf{Pareto frontier (quality vs.\ uplink).} Fig.~\ref{fig:pareto} plots how increasing the cumulative uplink traffic is caused by the improvement in model performance.
comments.explanation: This sentence claims that improving model performance CAUSES increased uplink traffic, which contradicts the paper's central thesis that reducing uplink traffic is the goal. The immediately following paragraph then contradicts this by saying Fed-FSTQ 'improves the communication--quality Pareto frontier.' The two descriptions of the same figure are mutually inconsistent.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of Fed-Aya Chinese results
comments.quote: Notably, for Chinese (\texttt{zh}), the 52\% reduction in cost from 4.35 to \textbf{2.08} is what enables the Fisher-guided allocation to identify semantically decisive tokens. This suggests that aggressive compression of low-sensitivity components is the prerequisite for the sensitivity signal to function.
comments.explanation: The reduction from 4.35 to 2.08 is approximately 52%, which is correct arithmetically. However, the causal interpretation is backwards: the paper claims the cost reduction 'enables' Fisher-guided allocation, but Fisher-guided allocation is the mechanism that produces the cost reduction. The method cannot be both the cause and the effect of its own improvement.
